papers.title: Well-posedness of stochastic heat equation with distributional drift and skew stochastic heat equation
papers.why_relevant: This is the foundational ABLM paper for the solution concept in the problem. Its approximation and stability theorems are essential for identifying mollified reversible equations with the limiting singular SPDE.

lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10
lemmas.statement: Fix T0>0. Let (D,p) be one of the three settings treated in the paper: D=R with the free heat kernel g_t(x−y); D=[0,1] with periodic heat kernel p_t^per; or D=[0,1] with Neumann heat kernel p_t^Neu. Let P_t be the corresponding heat semigroup, let W be space-time white noise on [0,T0]×D, and set V_t(x)=∫_0^t∫_D p_{t−r}(x,y) W(dr,dy). For a bounded continuous drift f:R→R and initial condition η∈B(D), write Eq(η;f) for the usual mild stochastic heat equation u_t(x)=P_t η(x)+∫_0^t∫_D p_{t−r}(x,y) f(u_r(y)) dy dr+V_t(x). Let B_q^β mean the nonhomogeneous Besov space B_{q,∞}^β(R). Say b_n→b in B_q^{β−} if sup_n ||b_n||_{B_q^β}<∞ and ||b_n−b||_{B_q^{β′}}→0 for every β′<β. Suppose β∈R, q∈[1,∞], β−1/q≥−1, β>−1, b∈B_q^β, and u0∈B(D). Let (b_n) be bounded continuous functions converging to b in B_q^{β−}. Let (u0^n)⊂B(D) converge uniformly on D to u0. Let u^n be a strong solution of Eq(u0^n;b_n), driven by the same white noise. Then there exists a measurable random field u:[0,T0]×D×Ω→R such that: (1) for every N>0, sup_{t∈(0,T0]} sup_{x∈D, |x|≤N} |u_t^n(x)−u_t(x)| → 0 in probability as n→∞; (2) u is a strong solution, in the paper's distributional-drift sense, of ∂_t u = (1/2)∂_{xx}u + b(u) + white noise with initial condition u0, i.e. there is a drift convolution K with u_t=P_tu0+K_t+V_t, u is continuous on (0,T0]×D, and for every smooth approximation c_j→b in B_q^{β−}, ∫_0^t∫_D p_{t−r}(x,y)c_j(u_r(y)) dy dr → K_t(x) uniformly over t∈[0,T0] and compact x-sets in probability; (3) for every m≥1, sup_{0<s<t≤T0} sup_{x∈D} ess sup_ω [ E( |u_t(x)−V_t(x)−P_{t−s}(u_s−V_s)(x)|^m | F_s )^{1/m} / |t−s|^{3/4} ] < ∞. In particular, the limiting solution belongs to the class V(3/4), i.e. its drift part has 3/4-Hölder increments in the heat-semigroup sense.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a Gyöngy–Krylov stability argument built on stochastic-sewing regularization estimates. First, by Besov embeddings the authors reduce to q≥2 and β<0. For smooth/continuous approximating drifts, Proposition 3.2 gives a uniform a priori bound on the drift part: [u^{η;f}−V]_{C^{1+β/4−1/(4q)},0 L^m,∞} ≤ C||f||_{B_q^β}. The estimate is proved by applying Lemma 5.2, whose core is the stochastic sewing lemma plus local nondeterminism of V; it is the regularization-by-noise input. Proposition 3.3 uses these uniform bounds and Kolmogorov/Arzela–Ascoli to prove tightness of pairs of centered approximations e u^n=u^n−P_tu0^n in C_uc([0,T0]×D). Proposition 3.4 is the identification step: if e u^n and the noises converge in C_uc, then the limit satisfies the distributional definition. The key decomposition compares K^{c;u}, K^{c;u^n}, and K^{b_n;u^n}; Lemma 5.4 controls these uniformly in space-time and in Besov norms, while the convergence b_n→b in lower Besov norms removes the approximation error. Now take any two subsequences of e u^n. By tightness and Skorohod, a further sub-subsequence has a weak limit pair (v′,v″) driven by the same limiting noise. Corollary 3.5 and Proposition 3.4 show both are weak solutions to the same singular equation and satisfy the V(3/4)-type bounds. Proposition 3.6, the uniqueness theorem, shows v′=v″ a.s.; its proof uses a rough-path-like expansion with H_{s,t} terms and the critical stochastic sewing lemma to control z=v′−v″ and force z≡0 by a Davie-type logarithmic Grönwall argument. The Gyöngy–Krylov criterion (Proposition 3.7) then implies the whole sequence e u^n converges in probability in C_uc to a single limit e u. Uniform convergence of P_tu0^n to P_tu0 gives the stated convergence of u^n. Since each u^n is F_t^W-measurable and convergence is in probability, the limit is strong. The conditional 3/4 estimate is inherited from Proposition 3.4. The paper states the theorem only for free, periodic, and Neumann kernels because its local nondeterminism bound is uniform in x there; a Dirichlet version would require replacing those kernel estimates, which is exactly the type of extension assumed in the original problem.

lemmas.label: Theorem 2.8 and Corollary 2.9
lemmas.statement: In the same three heat-kernel settings as above, consider the distributional-drift stochastic heat equation ∂_t u_t(x) = (1/2)∂_{xx}u_t(x) + b(u_t(x)) + white noise, with bounded measurable initial condition u0∈B(D), interpreted in the paper's sense: u_t=P_tu0+K_t+V_t, u is continuous on (0,T0]×D, and K is obtained as the probability limit of the heat-kernel convolutions of smooth Besov approximations of b. If b is a finite nonnegative Radon measure on R, then for every bounded initial condition u0 the equation has a unique strong solution, with no additional assumption that the solution belongs to V(3/4). In particular, for every κ∈R, the skew stochastic heat equation corresponding to b=κδ_0 has a unique strong solution for every bounded measurable initial condition u0.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Finite measures belong to B_1^0. Therefore Theorem 2.6(ii) gives existence and uniqueness among solutions in the class V(3/4), because β=0,q=1 gives β−1/q=−1 and β>−1. The extra work is to prove that, for nonnegative measure drift, every solution automatically lies in V(3/4); this is Proposition 3.8. Choose nonnegative smooth bounded approximations b_n→b in B_1^{0−} with ||b_n||_{B_1^0} uniformly bounded. Let K^n_t(x)=∫_0^t∫ p_{t−r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y))dy dr; by the solution definition K^n→K locally uniformly in probability, where u=P_tu0+K+V. Positivity of b_n gives positivity/monotonicity for the limiting drift: for 0≤s≤t≤T, P_{T−t}K_t(x) ≥ P_{T−s}K_s(x). This monotonicity is the substitute for an a priori drift regularity assumption. Next define, for fixed T,x, A_{s,t}^{T,n}(x)=∫_s^t∫ p_{T−r}(x,y)b_n(V_r(y)+P_{r−s}(u_s−V_s)(y))dy dr. Lemma 5.1 gives ||A_{s,t}^{T,n}(x)||_{L^m} ≤ C||b||_{B_1^0}|t−s|^{3/4}. The conditional increment δA is controlled by the random control λ_{s,t}^T(x)=P_{T−t}K_t(x)−P_{T−s}K_s(x), using positivity, heat-kernel smoothing, and the bound ||G_ρ b_n||_{C^1}≤C||b_n||_{B_1^0}ρ^{-1/2}. The stochastic sewing lemma with random controls (Theorem 4.7) then yields K_t^n(x)−P_{t−s}K_s^n(x) ≤ C[K_t(x)−P_{t−s}K_s(x)]|t−s|^{1/2}+L_{s,t}^n(x), with ||L_{s,t}^n(x)||_{L^m}≤C|t−s|^{3/4}. Passing n→∞ gives the same inequality for K. For |t−s| small, absorb the first term to obtain ||K_t−P_{t−s}K_s||_{L^m}≤C|t−s|^{3/4}; patching intervals gives u−V∈V(3/4). Hence the V(3/4)-uniqueness from Theorem 2.6 applies to all solutions. For κδ_0, the nonnegative case follows directly when κ≥0; the negative case follows by the symmetry u↦−u together with W↦−W (or equivalently by applying the positive case to −κ and reflecting), and κ=0 is the additive stochastic heat equation.

lemmas.label: Corollary 2.11
lemmas.statement: Fix β∈R and q∈[1,∞] with β−1/q≥−1 and β>−1. In one of the paper's three heat-kernel settings, let b′,b″∈B_q^β and u0′,u0″∈B(D). Let u′ and u″ be the strong distributional-drift solutions, driven by the same white noise, with respective drifts b′,b″ and initial conditions u0′,u0″. Suppose u0′(x)≤u0″(x) for Lebesgue-a.e. x∈D, and suppose b′≼b″ in the distributional order, meaning that ⟨b′,φ⟩≤⟨b″,φ⟩ for every nonnegative smooth test function φ; equivalently, b″−b′ is a nonnegative Radon measure. Then almost surely u′(t,x)≤u″(t,x) for all t>0 and all x∈D.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Approximate both drifts by heat mollification on R: b_n′=G_{1/n}b′ and b_n″=G_{1/n}b″. Lemma A.3 gives b_n′→b′ and b_n″→b″ in B_q^{β−}, and the heat kernel preserves the distributional order, so b_n′(x)≤b_n″(x) pointwise for every x. Let u_n′ and u_n″ be the classical mild solutions with smooth bounded drifts b_n′,b_n″, same driving white noise, and initial data u0′,u0″. For such regular equations the standard SPDE comparison principle applies: ordered initial data and pointwise ordered drifts imply u_n′(t,x)≤u_n″(t,x) for all t>0,x∈D almost surely. This is cited from the classical comparison results of Buckdahn–Pardoux and Gyöngy–Pardoux. The stability theorem, Theorem 2.10, gives locally uniform convergence in probability of u_n′ to u′ and of u_n″ to u″. Extract an almost surely locally uniformly convergent subsequence and pass to the limit in the pointwise inequalities. Continuity of the limiting random fields upgrades the inequality from a countable dense set to all t>0 and x∈D.

papers.title: Small Ball Probabilities and a Support Theorem for the Stochastic Heat Equation
papers.why_relevant: The support and small-ball estimates are useful for proving irreducibility or a common support point for the singular SPDE. The heat-kernel and additive-noise arguments are likely adaptable to Dirichlet boundary conditions.

lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
lemmas.statement: Support theorem with quantitative small-ball bounds. Let J≥1 and identify the endpoints of [0,J], so the spatial domain is the circle of length J. Let W be d-dimensional space-time white noise and let u(t,x)∈R^d be the mild solution of ∂_t u(t,x)=1/2 ∂_x²u(t,x)+g(t,x,u(t,x))+σ(t,x,u(t,x)) Ẇ(t,x), x∈[0,J], t≥0, with initial profile u(0,x)=u0(x). Assume g is uniformly bounded; σ:R_+×[0,J]×R^d→M_d(R) is symmetric-matrix-valued and Lipschitz in its third variable with Lipschitz constant D, i.e. |σ(t,x,z)-σ(t,x,z′)|≤D|z-z′|; and σ is uniformly elliptic: there are constants 0<a≤b<∞ such that a≤⟨y,σ(t,x,z)y⟩≤b for all t,x,z and all unit vectors y∈R^d. Let PC_b² denote the predictable functions h(t,x,ω) such that, almost surely, h, ∂_t h, and ∂_x²h are uniformly bounded by a deterministic constant H. Suppose u0 and h have this regularity, with u0 treated as time-independent, and suppose sup_{x∈[0,J]} |u0(x)-h(0,x)|<ε/2 almost surely. Then the following hold. (a) There exists D0=D0(J,a,b)>0 and positive constants A0,A1,A2,A3 depending only on d,a,b, and an ε0 depending additionally on H and sup_{t,x,z}|g(t,x,z)|, such that for every D<D0, every 0<ε<ε0, and every T>0, A0 exp(−A1 TJ/ε^6) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)−h(t,x)|≤ε) ≤ A2 exp(−A3 TJ/ε^6). (b) For any D and any δ∈(0,1), there are positive constants A0,A1,A2,A3 depending only on d,a,b, and an ε0 depending additionally on J,D,δ,H and sup_{t,x,z}|g(t,x,z)|, such that for every 0<ε<ε0 and every T>0, A0 exp(−A1 T J^{1+δ/2}/ε^{6+δ}) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)−h(t,x)|≤ε) ≤ A2 exp(−A3 TJ/((1+JD²)ε^6)).
lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is reduced to the zero-path small-ball estimate, Theorem 1.1, by subtracting the target path and the initial mismatch. Let H_op=1/2 ∂_x²−∂_t. Put h0(x)=h(0,x) and w(t,x)=u(t,x)−u0(x)−h(t,x)+h0(x). Then w(0,x)=0. A direct calculation gives ∂_t w=1/2 ∂_x²w−g1(t,x,w)+σ1(t,x,w) Ẇ, where g1=−g(t,x,u)−H_op u0−H_op h+H_op h0 and σ1(t,x,w)=σ(t,x,u). The hypotheses h,u0∈PC_b² ensure that g1 is uniformly bounded by a deterministic constant depending on H and sup|g|. The Lipschitz and ellipticity constants of σ1 are exactly those of σ, because u is obtained from w by adding a fixed predictable function. Thus Theorem 1.1 applies to w. The initial closeness assumption gives |u−h|≤|w|+|u0−h0|. Hence the lower bound follows by applying Theorem 1.1 to the event sup|w|≤ε/2, and the upper bound follows from {sup|u−h|≤ε}⊂{sup|w|≤3ε/2}; the factors 1/2 and 3/2 are absorbed into the constants. The proof uses no further structure of h except bounded H_op h and H_op h0, and the same argument works for predictable random h because all coefficients remain predictable and uniformly bounded.

lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
lemmas.statement: Global small-ball probabilities for the stochastic heat equation on the circle. Let J≥1 and identify the endpoints of [0,J]. Let W be d-dimensional space-time white noise and let u(t,x)∈R^d be the mild solution of ∂_t u(t,x)=1/2 ∂_x²u(t,x)+g(t,x,u(t,x))+σ(t,x,u(t,x)) Ẇ(t,x), x∈[0,J], t≥0, with zero initial condition u(0,x)≡0. Assume g is uniformly bounded; σ is symmetric-matrix-valued, Lipschitz in u with Lipschitz constant D; and σ is uniformly elliptic in the sense that there exist 0<a≤b<∞ with a≤⟨y,σ(t,x,z)y⟩≤b for every unit y∈R^d and every t,x,z. Then: (a) There exists D0=D0(J,a,b)>0 and positive constants A0,A1,A2,A3 depending only on d,a,b, and an ε0 depending additionally on sup_{t,x,z}|g(t,x,z)|, such that for any D<D0, any 0<ε<ε0, and any T>0, A0 exp(−A1 TJ/ε^6) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)|≤ε) ≤ A2 exp(−A3 TJ/ε^6). (b) For any D and any δ∈(0,1), there are positive constants A0,A1,A2,A3 depending only on d,a,b, and an ε0 depending additionally on J,D,δ and sup_{t,x,z}|g(t,x,z)|, such that for all 0<ε<ε0 and T>0, A0 exp(−A1 T J^{1+δ/2}/ε^{6+δ}) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)|≤ε) ≤ A2 exp(−A3 TJ/((1+JD²)ε^6)).
lemmas.proof_sketch: First remove the bounded drift g by Girsanov. Uniform ellipticity implies σ^{-1}g is uniformly bounded; shifting W by σ^{-1}g changes between the drifted and driftless equations, and the Radon-Nikodym derivative has second moment at most exp(C TJ). Cauchy-Schwarz transfers upper and lower bounds, changing only constants. Next reduce general J to J=1 by v(t,z)=J^{-1/2}u(J²t,Jz). The heat kernel and white noise scale so v solves the same equation on the unit circle with σ^J(t,z,r)=σ(J²t,Jz,J^{1/2}r), same ellipticity, and Lipschitz constant J^{1/2}D; this produces the factors J and 1+JD². For J=1,g=0, take time mesh t_n=n c0 ε^4 and space mesh x_j=j c1 ε², with c0,c1 chosen from heat-kernel covariance constants. Define F_n as the event that |u(t_n,x_j)|≤ε on the spatial grid, and A_n as the event that |u|≤ε throughout the slab [t_n,t_{n+1}] and |u(t_{n+1},·)|≤ε/3. The key Proposition 2.1 gives conditional bounds, uniformly in the past: P(F_n | previous F’s)≤C exp(−c/((1+D²)ε²)) and, for D small, P(A_n | previous A’s)≥C exp(−c/ε²). Multiplying over about T/ε^4 slabs by the Markov property gives exponents of order T/ε^6. For arbitrary D in the lower bound, the slab length is shortened to ε^{4+δ}, so the nonlinear error is negligible; multiplying over T/ε^{4+δ} slabs gives ε^{−6−δ}. The proof of Proposition 2.1 has two main parts. For deterministic σ, grid values at the next time are Gaussian with variances comparable to ε² and correlations decaying like exp(−θ(k−l)²/8); a linear-regression and ℓ¹ matrix inverse estimate gives a uniform positive conditional variance at each new grid point, hence a fixed chance to leave the ε-window. For the lower bound, a Girsanov control drives the initial profile to zero by the end of one slab; the stochastic convolution is kept small using local noise estimates and the Gaussian correlation inequality. For general Lipschitz σ, compare with the equation whose coefficient is frozen at the initial profile; the difference is a stochastic convolution with integrand O(Dε), controlled by the same local tail bound. Small D, or shorter time, ensures this comparison error is dominated by the Gaussian lower bound.

lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
lemmas.statement: Local tail bound for the stochastic convolution. Work on the unit circle [0,1] with endpoints identified, with g≡0. Let G(t,x)=∑_{n∈Z}(2πt)^{-1/2} exp(−(x+n)²/(2t)) be the heat kernel on the circle, and let u be a mild solution. Define the stochastic convolution term N(t,x)=∫_0^t∫_0^1 G(t−s,x−y) σ(s,y,u(s,y)) W(dy ds). Assume σ satisfies the uniform upper ellipticity bound from the paper, so in particular its operator norm is bounded by the constant b appearing in ⟨y,σ(t,x,z)y⟩≤b for unit y. Then there exist constants K1,K2>0 depending only on the dimension d such that, for all α>0, λ>0 and ε>0, P(sup_{0≤t≤α ε^4, x∈[0,ε²]} |N(t,x)|>λ ε) ≤ K1/(1∧√α) · exp(−K2 λ²/(b²√α)).
lemmas.proof_sketch: The input is a pair of sub-Gaussian increment estimates for N. Heat-kernel L² bounds on the circle give ∫[G(s,x−z)−G(s,y−z)]² dz ds≤C|x−y| and analogous time-increment bounds ≤C|t−s|^{1/2}. For each component of N, the corresponding stochastic integral is a continuous martingale; by time change to Brownian motion and the reflection principle, P(|N(t,x)−N(t,y)|>r)≤C exp(−c r²/(b²|x−y|)) and P(|N(t,x)−N(s,x)|>r)≤C exp(−c r²/(b²|t−s|^{1/2})). For α≥1, introduce parabolic dyadic grids G_n={(j/2^{2n},k/2^n):0≤j≤αε^4 2^{2n}, 0≤k≤ε²2^n} and start at n0=ceil(log_2(α^{-1/2}ε^{-2})). On level n there are O(2^{3(n−n0)}) nearest-neighbor edges. Let K=(4∑_{m≥0}2^{−m/4})^{-1}, and let A(n,λ) be the event that every nearest-neighbor increment on level n is at most λKε 2^{−(n−n0)/4}. The increment estimates plus a union bound give P(A(n,λ)^c)≤C 2^{3m} exp(−c λ² K² 2^{m/2}/(b²√α)), m=n−n0, and summing in m yields P(∩_n A(n,λ)^c)≤C exp(−c λ²/(b²√α)), after enlarging constants for small λ. A chaining argument then connects any grid point in the rectangle to (0,0) using at most four nearest-neighbor moves per level; on ∩_n A(n,λ), |N(t,x)|≤4∑_m λKε2^{−m/4}=λε. Continuity extends the bound from grid points to the whole rectangle. For 0<α<1, split [0,ε²] into about α^{-1/2} intervals of length √α ε² and apply the α=1 estimate at spatial scale √α ε² and time scale αε^4, with threshold λ ε=(λ/α^{1/4})(α^{1/4}ε); a union bound gives the prefactor 1/√α and the same exponential. The paper also notes the same proof applies to stochastic convolutions whose integrand is bounded by Cε, with the denominator in the exponential multiplied by C²ε².

papers.title: A Skew Stochastic Heat Equation
papers.why_relevant: This is the closest model problem: it develops the Gibbs/Dirichlet-form construction for a stochastic heat equation with a Dirac/local-time type drift. Its proofs of closability, invariance, integration by parts, resolvent absolute continuity, and Mosco convergence are directly portable to the proposed uniqueness strategy.

lemmas.label: Propositions 2.7 and 2.5
lemmas.statement: Let H = L2(0,1), let μ be the law on H of the standard Brownian bridge from 0 to 0 on [0,1], and let f:R→R be bounded and of bounded variation. Set F(x)=∫_0^1 f(x_r)dr and ν(dx)=Z^{-1}exp(-F(x))μ(dx). Let A=(1/2)d^2/dθ^2 with Dirichlet boundary conditions, D(A)=W^{2,2}(0,1)∩W^{1,2}_0(0,1), and let Exp_A(H) be the linear span of 1, cos(<·,h>), sin(<·,h>) with h∈D(A). Let (E,D(E)) be the closure in L2(ν) of E(φ,ψ)=(1/2)∫_H <∇φ,∇ψ> dν, φ,ψ∈Exp_A(H), and let (P_t)_{t≥0} be the associated L2(ν)-semigroup with resolvent R_λ=∫_0^∞ e^{-λt}P_t dt. Then for every t>0, P_t:L2(ν)→L2(ν) is Hilbert-Schmidt. Consequently there exists p_t∈L2(ν⊗ν) such that P_t φ(x)=∫_H φ(y)p_t(x,y)ν(dy) for ν-a.e. x and every φ∈L2(ν). Moreover, if δ_n denotes the nondecreasing enumeration of the eigenvalues of -L, where L is the generator of E, and δ_n^0 denotes the corresponding enumeration for the Brownian-bridge Ornstein-Uhlenbeck form E_0 in L2(μ), then the proof gives constants c,C>0 such that c δ_n^0 ≤ δ_n ≤ C δ_n^0 for all n; in particular the zero eigenspace is one-dimensional, consisting of constants. Finally, for every λ>0 there is a measurable kernel ρ_λ(x,dy), x∈H, representing the resolvent in the sense that R_λ φ(x)=∫_H φ(y)ρ_λ(x,dy) for ν-a.e. x and all φ∈L2(ν), and satisfying ρ_λ(x,dy)≪ν(dy) for every x∈H.
lemmas.proof_sketch: For the Brownian-bridge Ornstein-Uhlenbeck form E_0, the Hermite-chaos basis H_γ diagonalizes the generator, with eigenvalues Λ_γ=∑_{k≥1}γ_k π^2 k^2. Euler's product gives ∑_γ exp(-2tΛ_γ)=∏_{k≥1}(1-exp(-2tπ^2k^2))^{-1}<∞ for every t>0, so the OU semigroup is Hilbert-Schmidt. Since f is bounded, the density dν/dμ is bounded above and below; hence the L2 norms and the E_1 norms for E and E_0 are equivalent. Compactness of the embedding D(E_0)→L2(μ) transfers to D(E)→L2(ν), so the resolvent of E is compact. Applying the minimax principle to -L_0 and -L, and using equivalence of Rayleigh quotients, gives cδ_n^0≤δ_n≤Cδ_n^0. The exponential summability of δ_n follows from that of δ_n^0, proving P_t is Hilbert-Schmidt. A positive symmetric Hilbert-Schmidt operator on L2(ν) has an L2(ν⊗ν) kernel, giving p_t. For the resolvent absolute continuity, one uses the general quasi-regular Dirichlet-form theorem giving a properly exceptional set N and Markov kernels p_t(x,dy) on N^c with quasi-continuous versions of P_tφ. The Hilbert-Schmidt kernel implies p_t(x,dy)=p_t(x,y)ν(dy) for ν-a.e. x. Define ρ_λ(x,y)=∫_0^∞ e^{-λt}p_t(x,y)dt for ν⊗ν-a.e. (x,y). Thus R_λ has an absolutely continuous kernel for ν-a.e. x. To upgrade to all x outside N, take B with ν(B)=0; R_λ1_B is quasi-continuous and zero ν-a.e., hence by continuity on a closed nest and density it vanishes at every x∈N^c. On N the kernel is defined to be zero. This yields ρ_λ(x,·)≪ν for every x. The simple zero-eigenvalue consequence is the key ergodicity input: constants are the only L2(ν) functions fixed by the symmetric semigroup.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1, with Propositions 3.4–3.6
lemmas.statement: In the same setting H=L2(0,1), μ Brownian-bridge law, f bounded of bounded variation, F(x)=∫_0^1 f(x_r)dr, ν=Z^{-1}exp(-F)μ, and E the gradient Dirichlet form in L2(ν), the form (E,D(E)) is quasi-regular. The Markov process associated with this quasi-regular Dirichlet form is ν-symmetric, hence ν-invariant, and is a weak solution of the skew stochastic heat equation ∂_t u = (1/2)∂_θ^2u - (1/2)∫_R f(da) ∂_θ ℓ^a_{t,θ} + space-time white noise, with u(t,0)=u(t,1)=0. More explicitly, for the stationary process, and for every initial point outside a properly exceptional set in the nonstationary version, a.s. u∈C((0,T]×[0,1]) and for a.e. t the spatial path θ↦u(t,θ) has a bicontinuous family of local times ℓ^a_{t,θ}, a∈R, θ∈[0,1], satisfying the occupation formula ∫_0^θ g(u(t,r))dr=∫_R g(a)ℓ^a_{t,θ}da for bounded Borel g. There exists a Brownian sheet W such that for all h∈C_c^2((0,1)) and 0<ε≤t, <u_t-u_ε,h> = (1/2)∫_ε^t <h'',u_s>ds + (1/2)∫_ε^t∫_R f(da)∫_0^1 h'(θ)ℓ^a_{s,θ}dθ ds + ∫_ε^t∫_0^1 h(θ)W(ds,dθ). The integration-by-parts identity underlying the construction is: for h∈D(A) and φ∈C_b^1(H), E[ρ(β) ∂_h φ(β)] = E[ρ(β) φ(β)(-<h'',β> + ∫_{R×[0,1]} f(da) h_r ℓ^a(dr))], where β is a Brownian bridge, ρ=exp(-F), and ℓ^a(r) are its spatial local times.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Quasi-regularity transfers from the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Brownian-bridge form because f is bounded, so E_1 and E_{0,1} are equivalent. Existence of a Hunt process follows from the quasi-regular Dirichlet-form theory. Since ν is equivalent to μ, ν-a.e. spatial path is a Brownian-bridge path and has bicontinuous local times. The absolute-continuity property of the transition kernels implies the process spends zero Lebesgue time in the complement of this full-measure set, giving local times for a.e. time. The key integration by parts is proved by differentiating F(β+εh). Using the occupation formula, F(β+εh) can be written in terms of the local times of β, and d/dε at 0 equals -∫ f(da)h_rℓ^a(dr). Combining this with the Cameron-Martin formula for shifting the Brownian bridge by εh gives the displayed identity. Apply Fukushima's decomposition to the linear functional U_h(x)=<x,h>: U_h(X_t)-U_h(X_0)=M^{[U_h]}_t+N^{[U_h]}_t. The integration-by-parts formula identifies the Revuz measure of the zero-energy CAF N^{[U_h]}; finite-energy/smoothness follows from bounded variation of f, boundedness of h, and Lp bounds on Brownian local times. This identifies N as the sum of the heat drift (1/2)∫<h'',u_s>ds and the local-time drift with h'. The martingale part is identified by the carré-du-champ formula: its quadratic variation has Revuz measure ||h||_H^2ν, hence <M^h>_t=||h||^2t, and cross-variations are t<h_1,h_2>. Therefore h↦M^h is an isonormal martingale field, representable as integration against a Brownian sheet. Continuity in (t,θ) is obtained by the paper's a priori Kolmogorov estimate, together with stationarity and the equivalence ν∼μ.

lemmas.label: Propositions 4.7 and 4.8
lemmas.statement: Let f:R→R be bounded and of bounded variation, with discontinuity set Δ_f, and let f_n be a smooth approximation as fixed in Section 4: the f_n are uniformly bounded, f_n→f locally uniformly on R\Δ_f, and the associated signed measures f_n'(a)da converge to the Stieltjes measure f(da) in the integrations used below. Define F_n(x)=∫_0^1 f_n(x_r)dr and ν_n(dx)=Z_n^{-1}exp(-F_n(x))μ(dx), where μ is Brownian-bridge law on H=L2(0,1). Let E_n be the closure in L2(ν_n) of E_n(φ,ψ)=(1/2)∫_H <∇φ,∇ψ>dν_n on Exp_A(H), and let E be the limiting form in L2(ν). Under the canonical identification of the Hilbert spaces L2(ν_n) with L2(ν), the forms E_n Mosco-converge to E. Consequently, by the Kuwae-Shioya theorem, the associated resolvents and semigroups converge strongly. If P^n_{ν_n} denotes the law of the stationary solution of the smooth stochastic heat equation ∂_t u^n=(1/2)∂_θ^2u^n-(1/2)f_n'(u^n)+space-time white noise, with Dirichlet boundary conditions and initial law ν_n, and P_ν denotes the law of the stationary Markov process associated with E, then for every T>0, P^n_{ν_n} converges weakly to P_ν on C([0,T]×[0,1]).
lemmas.proof_sketch: First identify all L2(ν_n) with L2(ν). Because the densities dν_n/dμ and dν/dμ are bounded above and below uniformly and dν_n/dμ→dν/dμ a.s. by dominated convergence, norms converge on fixed functions; strong and weak convergence in the varying Hilbert spaces are equivalent to the usual strong/weak convergence in L2(ν). Mosco II is immediate: for u∈D(E), take u_n=u and use dominated convergence of the energy densities; outside D(E) all energies are infinite. For Mosco I, assume u_n weakly converges to u and has bounded liminf energy. Testing against the core Exp_A(H), use the integration-by-parts formula for E and the smooth integration-by-parts formula for E_n. For v=exp(i<h,·>), the drift terms are i v times either ∫ f(da)h_rℓ^a(dr) or ∫ h_r f_n'(β_r)dr; weak convergence f_n'(a)da→f(da), the occupation formula, and dominated convergence identify the limits. Cauchy-Schwarz gives E(u,v)^2/E(v,v)≤liminf E_n(u_n,u_n); taking the supremum over core v yields E(u,u)≤liminf. If u∉D(E), bounded energy would imply compactness through comparison with the Brownian-bridge form and contradict closedness/lower semicontinuity. Mosco convergence then implies strong convergence of semigroups. For stationary processes, finite-dimensional distributions converge by iterating the Markov property and strong semigroup convergence. Tightness on C([0,T]×[0,1]) is obtained uniformly in n: bounded densities reduce spatial Sobolev moment bounds under ν_n to Brownian-bridge estimates, and the paper's Lemma 6.1 converts these stationary Sobolev bounds plus the Dirichlet-form martingale estimate into time Hölder moment bounds in C^θ([0,1]); Kolmogorov and Ethier-Kurtz compactness finish the weak convergence.

papers.title: A Theory of Hypoellipticity and Unique Ergodicity for Semilinear Stochastic PDEs
papers.why_relevant: It provides the asymptotic strong Feller plus irreducibility framework for uniqueness of invariant measures in infinite dimensions. Its gradient-estimate and noise-transfer arguments are a strong fallback if full strong Feller or pointwise kernel equivalence is too hard.

lemmas.label: Corollary 1.4
lemmas.statement: Let (P_t)_{t≥0} be a Markov semigroup on a Polish space X. Assume: (1) P_t is Feller, meaning P_t maps bounded continuous functions on X to bounded continuous functions on X for every t≥0; (2) P_t is asymptotically strong Feller; (3) P_t is weakly topologically irreducible, meaning that for every pair u_1,u_2 in X there exists v in X such that, for every open set A containing v, there exist times t_1,t_2>0 with P_{t_i}(u_i,A)>0 for i=1,2. Then P_t admits at most one invariant probability measure on X.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The key input is Theorem 1.2 of the paper: for an asymptotically strong Feller Markov semigroup on a Polish space, two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures must have disjoint topological supports. To prove the corollary, suppose two distinct invariant measures exist; by ergodic decomposition reduce to distinct ergodic invariant measures μ_1, μ_2. Pick u_i in supp μ_i. Weak topological irreducibility gives a common point v such that every neighborhood A of v is reachable with positive probability from both u_i, possibly at different times. The Feller property upgrades pointwise positivity to uniform positivity on a small neighborhood of u_i: if P_t(u_i,A)>0 and A is open, then by continuity of z ↦ P_t(z,A) from below using bounded continuous approximations to 1_A, there is an open ball B around u_i with inf_{z in B} P_t(z,A)>0. Since u_i lies in supp μ_i, μ_i(B)>0. Invariance gives μ_i(A)=∫ P_t(z,A) μ_i(dz) ≥ μ_i(B) inf_B P_t(·,A)>0. Applying this to every ball A around v shows v belongs to supp μ_1 ∩ supp μ_2. This contradicts Theorem 1.2, so there cannot be two invariant probability measures.

lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1
lemmas.statement: Let (P_t)_{t≥0} be a Markov semigroup on a Hilbert space H. Let (t_n) be a nondecreasing sequence of positive times and let (δ_n) be a positive sequence with δ_n→0. Suppose there is a fixed nondecreasing function C:R_+→R_+ such that, for every bounded Fréchet differentiable function phi:H→R with bounded derivative, every n, and every u in H, one has ||D(P_{t_n}phi)(u)|| ≤ C(||u||)(||phi||_∞ + δ_n ||Dphi||_∞). Then P_t is asymptotically strong Feller. More explicitly, one may use totally separating bounded metrics d_n(x,y)=1∧(||x−y||/sqrt(δ_n)) and their induced 1-Wasserstein distances: for every u in H, inf over neighborhoods U of u of limsup_{n→∞} sup_{v in U} d_n(P_{t_n}(u,·),P_{t_n}(v,·)) equals 0.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose the metrics d_n(x,y)=1∧(||x−y||/sqrt(δ_n)); since δ_n→0, d_n(x,y)→1 for x≠y, so the metrics separate points asymptotically and the corresponding Wasserstein distances approach total variation in the usual sense. If a test function f is 1-Lipschitz for d_n and bounded by 1, then at the Hilbert-space scale its Lipschitz constant is at most δ_n^{-1/2}. Applying the assumed gradient estimate to such f gives ||D P_{t_n}f(z)|| ≤ C(||z||)(1+sqrt(δ_n)) for smooth f; standard approximation/duality extends the estimate to all bounded d_n-Lipschitz tests. For z on the segment between u and v in a small ball around u, C(||z||) is uniformly bounded by C(||u||+r). Integrating the derivative of P_{t_n}f along the segment yields |P_{t_n}f(u)−P_{t_n}f(v)| ≤ C(||u||+r)(1+sqrt(δ_n))||u−v||. Taking the supremum over all d_n-Lipschitz f gives the same bound for the Wasserstein distance d_n(P_{t_n}(u,·),P_{t_n}(v,·)). Now take sup over ||v−u||<r, limsup in n, and then r↓0. This is precisely the asymptotic strong Feller condition.

lemmas.label: Theorem 5.5
lemmas.statement: Let H be a Hilbert space and consider the semilinear additive-noise SPDE du_t = −L u_t dt + N(u_t) dt + G dW_t, u_0=u, where W is a standard R^d Brownian motion, G e_k=g_k, and P_t phi(u)=E_u phi(u_t). Assume the analytic-polynomial setup of Assumption A.1: L is selfadjoint, ⟨u,Lu⟩≥||u||², and its interpolation spaces are H_α; there are a in [0,1) and γ⋆,β⋆>−a with γ⋆+β⋆>−1 such that N is a polynomial map of degree at most n, continuous H_{γ+a}→H_γ for every γ in [−a,γ⋆); for every β in [−a,β⋆) there is γ in [0,γ⋆+1) such that DN*(u) extends to a continuous map H_γ→L(H_{β+a},H_β); and g_k belongs to H_{γ⋆+1}. Assume global mild solutions exist. Let J_{s,t} be the derivative of the solution map with respect to initial data, J^{(2)}_{s,t} its second variation, and M_{s,t}=∫_s^t J_{r,t} G G* J_{r,t}* dr the Malliavin covariance operator; write M_1=M_{0,1}. Suppose the following hold. B1: there are a function U:H→[1,∞), an orthogonal projection Π, a number qbar≥2, and C_U such that for every α>0, p≥1, ε≤1, and u, P_u(inf_{||Πh||≥α||h||} ⟨h,M_1 h⟩/||h||² ≤ ε) ≤ C(α,p) U(u)^p ε^p, and E_u U(u_n)^qbar ≤ C_U^qbar U(u)^qbar uniformly over integers n≥0. B2: there are V:H→R_+, C_L>0, and η′ in [0,1) with E_u exp(V(u_1)) ≤ exp(η′ V(u)+C_L). B3: for some pbar≥10 and η>0 with pbar η<1−η′ and 2/qbar+10/pbar≤1, there are constants C_J and C_J^{(2)} such that for all 0≤s≤t≤1, E_u ||J_{s,t}||^pbar ≤ exp(pbar η V(u)+pbar C_J) and E_u ||J^{(2)}_{s,t}||^pbar ≤ exp(pbar η V(u)+pbar C_J^{(2)}). B4: with Π⊥=I−Π, E_u ||J_{0,1} Π⊥||^pbar ≤ exp(pbar η V(u)−pbar C_Π), where C_Π−C_J>2κ C_L and κ=η/(1−η′). Then, for every ζ in [0,(C_Π−C_J)/2−κ C_L), setting γ=e^{−ζ}, there is C<∞ such that for every integer n≥0 and every test function phi for which the displayed quantities are finite, ||D(P_{2n}phi)(u)|| ≤ exp(4κ V(u)) [ C U(u)^2 sqrt(P_{2n}(phi^2)(u)) + γ^{2n} sqrt(P_{2n}(||Dphi||^2)(u)) ]. Here P_{2n}(||Dphi||^2)(u)=E_u sup_{||ξ||=1}|Dphi(u_{2n})ξ|².
lemmas.proof_sketch: Differentiate the Markov semigroup in an initial direction ξ: D_ξ P_t phi(u)=E[D phi(u_t) J_{0,t}ξ]. The central device is to transfer most of the initial variation J_{0,t}ξ into a Malliavin variation of the driving Brownian path. For a Cameron-Martin control h, Malliavin integration by parts gives D_ξ P_t phi=E[phi(u_t)∫ h dW]+E[D phi(u_t)ρ_t], where ρ_t=J_{0,t}ξ−A_{0,t}h and A_{s,t}h=∫_s^t J_{r,t}G h_r dr. Thus one needs h with bounded Skorokhod cost and ρ_t decaying. The control is chosen blockwise. On even intervals [2k,2k+1], set h=A_{2k}*(M_{2k}+β_{2k})^{-1}J_{2k}ρ_{2k}; on odd intervals set h=0. Then ρ_{2k+2}=J_{2k+1} R_{2k}^{β} J_{2k}ρ_{2k}, where R^{β}=β(M+β)^{-1}. Assumption B1 says M is unlikely to be small on vectors with a nontrivial Π-component. Choosing β_k proportional to δ³ divided by U(u_k) yields E(||Π R_k^{β}||^p | F_k) ≤ const δ^p. Split the one-step error operator as JΠ⊥ R J + JΠ R J. The first part is controlled by the deterministic smoothing/contraction B4; the second is made small by the Malliavin lower bound. Lyapunov weights χ_k=exp(ηV(u_{2k})+ηV(u_{2k+1})) and B2-B3 control growth of J along the blocks. Iteration gives E||ρ_{2n}||^p ≤ exp(pκV(u)−p rate n)||ξ||^p for every rate below C_Π−C_J−2κC_L. It remains to bound ∫h dW. Since h is not adapted inside each block, the Skorokhod isometry is used: E|∫h dW|² is bounded by E||h||_{L²}² plus Malliavin-derivative correction terms. Differentiating h brings in derivatives of J and A, hence the second variation J^{(2)}; B3 controls these, B1 controls negative moments of β_k, and the already proved decay of ρ_k makes the block sum finite. This yields E|∫h dW|² ≤ C exp((8η+2κ)V(u))U(u)^2||ξ||². Cauchy-Schwarz applied to the two terms in the integration-by-parts identity gives the displayed gradient estimate.

papers.title: Invariant Measures for Nonlinear SPDE's: Uniqueness and Stability
papers.why_relevant: It gives a concise uniqueness mechanism from equivalence of positive-time transition kernels, exactly the kind of criterion needed after proving absolute continuity with respect to the Gibbs/Brownian-bridge reference law. It also treats nondegenerate Dirichlet heat noise examples.

lemmas.label: Theorem 4
lemmas.statement: Let H be a Polish space and let ((X_t)_{t≥0},(P^x)_{x∈H}) be a time-homogeneous Markov process in H with transition probabilities P_t(x,B)=P^x(X_t∈B). Suppose that this Markov process has an invariant probability measure µ_*, i.e. ∫_H P_t(x,B) µ_*(dx)=µ_*(B) for every t≥0 and every Borel set B⊆H. Assume that all positive-time transition probabilities are mutually equivalent: for any s,t>0 and any x,y∈H, the probability measures P_t(x,·) and P_s(y,·) are mutually absolutely continuous. Then: (i) for every bounded Borel function φ:H→R and every x∈H, lim_{T→∞} (1/T)∫_0^T φ(X_t) dt = ∫_H φ dµ_* holds P^x-a.s.; (ii) for every x∈H, ∥P_t(x,·)−µ_*∥_{TV}→0 as t→∞. In particular, the invariant probability measure µ_* is unique.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as the Stettner-Seidler/Doob type theorem; the key mechanism is as follows. First fix h>0 and write K(x,·)=P_h(x,·). Invariance plus mutual equivalence imply K(x,·)∼µ_* for every x: if K(x0,A)=0, equivalence gives K(y,A)=0 for all y, hence µ_*(A)=∫K(y,A)µ_*(dy)=0; conversely, if µ_*(A)=0 but K(x0,A)>0 for some x0, equivalence gives K(y,A)>0 for every y, so the invariant integral is positive, a contradiction. Thus the h-skeleton is a regular Markov chain with invariant probability µ_*. Moreover, if 0<µ_*(A)<1 then K(x,A)>0 and K(x,A^c)>0 for all x, so there are no nontrivial invariant sets or cyclic classes; finite invariance gives positive recurrence. The classical Doob theorem for regular positive recurrent Markov chains then yields K^n(x,·)→µ_* in total variation and the pointwise ergodic theorem for the skeleton. Continuous-time total variation convergence follows by writing t=nh+r, 0≤r<h, and using contraction of total variation under the Markov kernel P_r: ∥P_t(x,·)−µ_*∥=∥(P_{nh}(x,·)−µ_*)P_r∥≤∥P_{nh}(x,·)−µ_*∥. For the continuous-time ergodic average, decompose over unit intervals: ∫_0^N φ(X_s)ds=∑_{k=0}^{N-1} A_k with A_k=∫_k^{k+1}φ(X_s)ds. Let g(x)=E^x A_0. The skeleton ergodic theorem gives N^{-1}∑g(X_k)→∫g dµ_*=∫φ dµ_* by invariance; the differences A_k−g(X_k) are bounded martingale differences, so their Cesaro average tends to 0 by the martingale strong law; the terminal incomplete interval is negligible. If ν is any invariant probability, integrating the total-variation convergence against ν gives ν=µ_*.

lemmas.label: Lemma 6
lemmas.statement: Let H be a Polish space and let P_t(x,·), t≥0, x∈H, be the transition probabilities of a Markov process on H. Say the process is strong Feller if, for every t>0 and every Borel set Γ⊆H, the map x↦P_t(x,Γ) is continuous; equivalently, P_t maps bounded Borel functions to bounded continuous functions. Say the process is irreducible if P_t(x,U)>0 for every t>0, every x∈H, and every nonempty open set U⊆H. If the process is strong Feller and irreducible, then the measures P_t(x,·), t>0, x∈H, are mutually equivalent.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Irreducibility says each P_t(x,·) has full topological support. To prove absolute continuity, suppose P_t(x,A)=0 for some Borel A. Put a=t/2 and f(z)=P_a(z,A). By the strong Feller property, f is continuous and nonnegative. The Markov property gives 0=P_t(x,A)=∫ f(z)P_a(x,dz). If f(z0)>0 at some z0, continuity gives a nonempty open neighborhood U of z0 on which f is bounded below by a positive constant; irreducibility gives P_a(x,U)>0, contradicting the integral being zero. Hence P_a(z,A)=0 for every z. This zero-propagates to all later times by the semigroup identity: for r≥a, P_r(y,A)=∫P_a(z,A)P_{r-a}(y,dz)=0. It also propagates to earlier times: if 0<r<a and some z0 had P_r(z0,A)>0, then by strong Feller there would be a nonempty open U where P_r(·,A)>c>0; irreducibility for time a−r would give P_a(y,A)=∫P_r(z,A)P_{a-r}(y,dz)>0, contradicting P_a(y,A)=0. Therefore P_r(y,A)=0 for every r>0 and y∈H. Interchanging the roles of the two transition measures gives mutual absolute continuity.

lemmas.label: Theorem 5
lemmas.statement: Let H be a real separable Hilbert space. Let A:Dom(A)⊆H→H generate a strongly continuous semigroup (e^{At})_{t≥0}. Let W_t be a Wiener process on H with incremental covariance operator Q∈L(H), possibly cylindrical, and assume that ∫_0^T ∥e^{At}Q^{1/2}∥_{HS}^2 dt<∞ for some T>0. For t≥0 set Q_t=∫_0^t e^{Ar}Qe^{A^*r}dr. Consider the linear stochastic evolution equation dZ_t=AZ_t dt+dW_t and the semilinear equation dX_t=AX_t dt+f(X_t)dt+dW_t. Let ~P_t and P_t denote their transition probability functions, respectively. Assume the range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) for every t>0. Assume also that f:H→Im(Q^{1/2}) satisfies ∥Q^{-1/2}(f(x)−f(y))∥≤K∥x−y∥ for some K<∞ and all x,y∈H. Then P_t(x,·) and ~P_t(x,·) are equivalent probability measures for every t>0 and x∈H. If, moreover, the semilinear equation has an invariant probability measure µ_*, then ∥P_t(x,·)−µ_*∥_{TV}→0 as t→∞ for every x∈H; in particular µ_* is the unique invariant probability measure.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The linear transition laws are Gaussian: ~P_t(x,·)=N(e^{At}x,Q_t). The range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) is precisely the Cameron-Martin/Hájek-Feldman condition ensuring that the Gaussian transition probabilities of the linear equation at positive times are mutually equivalent. Next compare the nonlinear and linear path laws on a finite interval [0,T]. For a linear solution Z, the Cameron-Martin-valued drift β_s=Q^{-1/2}f(Z_s) is progressively measurable and has at most linear growth because of the displayed Lipschitz condition. The Girsanov density is M_T=exp{∫_0^T ⟨Q^{-1/2}f(Z_s),dW_s⟩ − (1/2)∫_0^T ∥Q^{-1/2}f(Z_s)∥^2 ds}; the hypotheses give the required integrability/Novikov condition. Under the tilted measure, the shifted noise has the same covariance and Z solves the semilinear mild equation; applying the inverse change of drift gives mutual absolute continuity of the two path laws. Projection to time t yields P_t(x,·)∼~P_t(x,·). Combining this with the linear Gaussian equivalence gives mutual equivalence of the nonlinear positive-time transition kernels. The final assertion is then exactly Theorem 4. The spectral calculation in the paper's Example 3 is a standard way to verify the range condition for nondegenerate parabolic heat noise.
